FI 362 — Chapter 2 Supplement

Linear Time Series Analysis, Step by Step on Real Data

Author

Mehman Ismayilli

Published

June 28, 2026

How to use this notebook. It mirrors the Chapter 2 slides (FI362_Ch2) section by section, but computes every concept on real market data instead of stating it abstractly. Read the slide, then run the matching block here. All data lives in the sibling data/ folder built by data/download_data.R (daily Yahoo Finance prices, Jan 2000 – mid 2026, adjusted for splits & dividends). No internet is needed to run this file.

Where it adds value, we hand-code the core formula (sample ACF, Ljung–Box, Yule–Walker, the ADF regression, the GPH long-memory estimator) and then cross-check it against the standard package. Heavier packages (forecast, urca, fracdiff, TSA) are used only when installed — each block degrades gracefully.

1 Setup & data

library(knitr)

# Optional packages: we use them if present, but never require them.
have <- function(p) requireNamespace(p, quietly = TRUE)
pk <- c(forecast = have("forecast"), urca = have("urca"),
        fracdiff = have("fracdiff"), TSA = have("TSA"),
        sandwich = have("sandwich"), lmtest = have("lmtest"))
kable(data.frame(package = names(pk), installed = unname(pk)),
      caption = "Optional packages detected on this machine.")
Optional packages detected on this machine.
package installed
forecast TRUE
urca TRUE
fracdiff TRUE
TSA FALSE
sandwich FALSE
lmtest TRUE 
# Locate data/ robustly (works from this folder, the project root, or RStudio).
data_dir <- local({
  cands <- c("../data", "data", file.path(getwd(), "data"),
             file.path(dirname(getwd()), "data"))
  hit <- cands[file.exists(file.path(cands, "indices_adjclose.csv"))]
  if (!length(hit)) stop("Could not find data/. Run data/download_data.R first.")
  hit[1]
})

read_prices <- function(file) {
  df <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, file), check.names = FALSE)
  df$Date <- as.Date(df$Date); df
}

indices   <- read_prices("indices_adjclose.csv")   # daily, wide (11 indices)
stocks    <- read_prices("stocks_adjclose.csv")    # daily, wide (5 US stocks)
idx_month <- read_prices("indices_monthly.csv")    # month-end, wide
sp_month  <- read_prices("sp500_monthly.csv")      # month-end S&P 500
meta      <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "metadata.csv"))

kable(meta[, c("symbol","name","region","asset_class","rows","start","end")],
      caption = "The universe of global indices and US stocks.")
The universe of global indices and US stocks.
symbol name region asset_class rows start end
^GSPC SP500 United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^IXIC NASDAQ United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^DJI DowJones United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^FTSE FTSE100 United Kingdom Equity index 6719 2000-01-04 2026-05-29
^GDAXI DAX Germany Equity index 6747 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^FCHI CAC40 France Equity index 6784 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^N225 Nikkei225 Japan Equity index 6589 2000-01-04 2026-06-01
^HSI HangSeng Hong Kong Equity index 6583 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
000001.SS SSEComposite China Equity index 6519 2000-01-04 2026-05-29
^KS11 KOSPI South Korea Equity index 6603 2000-01-04 2026-06-01
^BSESN Sensex India Equity index 6596 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
AAPL AAPL United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
MSFT MSFT United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
AXP AXP United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
CAT CAT United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
SBUX SBUX United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29 
# --- returns -----------------------------------------------------------------
log_ret <- function(p) { p <- p[is.finite(p)]; diff(log(p)) }   # r_t = ln P_t - ln P_{t-1}

# month-end series from a daily (Date, price) frame
to_monthly <- function(date, price) {
  ok <- is.finite(price); date <- date[ok]; price <- price[ok]
  key <- format(date, "%Y-%m")
  idx <- tapply(seq_along(price), key, max)        # last obs in each month
  data.frame(Date = date[idx], Price = price[idx])
}

# --- sample ACF EXACTLY as on the slides -------------------------------------
samp_acf <- function(x, lag.max) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x); xb <- mean(x)
  g0 <- sum((x - xb)^2)
  sapply(0:lag.max, function(l)
    sum((x[(l+1):T] - xb) * (x[1:(T-l)] - xb)) / g0)
}

# --- Ljung–Box Q(m) by hand --------------------------------------------------
ljung_box <- function(x, m) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x)
  r <- samp_acf(x, m)[-1]                          # drop lag 0
  Q <- T * (T + 2) * sum(r^2 / (T - (1:m)))
  data.frame(m = m, Q = Q, df = m, p_value = 1 - pchisq(Q, m))
}

# running example: S&P 500 (index) and AAPL (stock)
# keep only rows with a finite S&P price so dates and returns stay aligned
# (the 11 global indices share one calendar, so SP500 has gaps on non-US sessions)
.ok_sp <- is.finite(indices$SP500)
sp_px  <- data.frame(Date = indices$Date[.ok_sp], Price = indices$SP500[.ok_sp])
sp_d   <- log_ret(sp_px$Price)                     # daily S&P 500 log returns
sp_m   <- log_ret(sp_month$SP500)                  # monthly S&P 500 log returns
aapl_d <- log_ret(stocks$AAPL)                     # daily AAPL log returns
aapl_m <- log_ret(to_monthly(stocks$Date, stocks$AAPL)$Price)

We use the S&P 500 as the running index and AAPL as the running stock, but every computation is column-agnostic — swap "SP500" for "DAX", "AAPL" for "MSFT", etc.

2 Stationarity

Slides §2.1. Weak stationarity = constant mean and an autocovariance that depends only on the lag. A stationary series fluctuates around a fixed level with constant spread; a non-stationary one wanders.

The cleanest demonstration is the same asset one difference apart: the price wanders (non-stationary), the return is anchored at zero (stationary).

op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2, 1))
plot(sp_px$Date, log(sp_px$Price), type = "l", col = "tomato",
     xlab = "", ylab = "log price", main = "S&P 500 log price — wandering level (non-stationary)")
plot(sp_px$Date[-1], 100 * sp_d, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "", ylab = "log return (%)", main = "S&P 500 log return — fixed level, ~constant spread (stationary)")
abline(h = 0, col = "grey40"); par(op)

A quick numerical check: split the return series in half and compare moments. For a stationary series the two halves should look alike; for the price they do not.

half <- function(x) { x <- x[is.finite(x)]; n <- length(x)
  data.frame(first_mean = mean(x[1:(n%/%2)]),  second_mean = mean(x[(n%/%2+1):n]),
             first_sd   = sd(x[1:(n%/%2)]),    second_sd   = sd(x[(n%/%2+1):n])) }
kable(rbind(`log price` = half(log(sp_px$Price)),
            `log return` = half(sp_d)),
      digits = 4, caption = "Mean/SD across the two halves of the sample.")
Mean/SD across the two halves of the sample.
first_mean second_mean first_sd second_sd
log price 7.0849 8.0644 0.1623 0.4173
log return 0.0000 0.0005 0.0134 0.0108

3 Correlation and the ACF

Slides §2.2. \(\rho_\ell=\gamma_\ell/\gamma_0\). Under i.i.d., \(\hat\rho_\ell\sim N(0,1/T)\), so bands are \(\pm1.96/\sqrt T\). The Ljung–Box statistic \(Q(m)=T(T+2)\sum_{\ell=1}^m\hat\rho_\ell^2/(T-\ell)\to\chi^2_m\).

Hand-coded vs built-in ACF — they agree to machine precision:

hand <- samp_acf(sp_d, 10)[-1]
base <- as.numeric(acf(sp_d, lag.max = 10, plot = FALSE)$acf)[-1]
kable(data.frame(lag = 1:10, hand_coded = hand, base_acf = base,
                 diff = hand - base), digits = 6,
      caption = "Sample ACF of daily S&P 500 returns: our formula vs acf().")
Sample ACF of daily S&P 500 returns: our formula vs acf().
lag hand_coded base_acf diff
1 -0.099121 -0.099121 0
2 -0.003864 -0.003864 0
3 0.003236 0.003236 0
4 -0.025729 -0.025729 0
5 -0.010560 -0.010560 0
6 -0.031045 -0.031045 0
7 0.026879 0.026879 0
8 -0.026409 -0.026409 0
9 0.038380 0.038380 0
10 -0.000140 -0.000140 0

Frequency changes the verdict. Daily index returns carry a clear negative lag-1 autocorrelation (bid–ask bounce / micro mean-reversion); monthly returns are indistinguishable from white noise.

op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
acf(sp_d,   lag.max = 30, main = "Daily S&P 500 returns")
acf(aapl_d, lag.max = 30, main = "Daily AAPL returns")
acf(sp_m,   lag.max = 24, main = "Monthly S&P 500 returns")
acf(aapl_m, lag.max = 24, main = "Monthly AAPL returns"); par(op)

Ljung–Box, hand-coded vs Box.test:

lb <- rbind(
  cbind(series = "S&P daily",   ljung_box(sp_d, 10)),
  cbind(series = "S&P monthly", ljung_box(sp_m, 5)),
  cbind(series = "S&P monthly", ljung_box(sp_m, 12)),
  cbind(series = "AAPL daily",  ljung_box(aapl_d, 10)))
kable(lb, digits = 4, caption = "Ljung–Box (hand-coded).")
Ljung–Box (hand-coded).
series m Q df p_value
S&P daily 10 96.2229 10 0.0000
S&P monthly 5 5.6827 5 0.3383
S&P monthly 12 15.8829 12 0.1967
AAPL daily 10 35.3402 10 0.0001 
# cross-check one of them against the base function
c(hand = ljung_box(sp_d, 10)$p_value,
  base = Box.test(sp_d, lag = 10, type = "Ljung-Box")$p.value)
        hand         base 
3.330669e-16 3.330669e-16

Read-off: daily returns reject “no serial correlation” decisively (\(p<10^{-15}\)); monthly returns do not (\(p\approx0.20\)). Markets are closer to efficient at the monthly horizon.

4 White noise & linear time series

Slides §2.3. White noise \(\{a_t\}\) is i.i.d. with mean 0, finite variance, all ACFs zero. A linear series is \(r_t=\mu+\sum_{i\ge0}\psi_i a_{t-i}\) with \(\psi_0=1\); the \(\psi\)-weights are the impulse response and give the variance, ACF, and forecast-error variance.

set.seed(1)
a <- rnorm(500)                                    # Gaussian white noise
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(a, type = "l", col = "grey40", main = "White noise", xlab = "t", ylab = expression(a[t]))
acf(a, lag.max = 20, main = "ACF ~ 0 at all lags"); par(op)

The \(\psi\)-weights make AR/MA/ARMA a single family. For an AR(1) with \(\phi=0.6\), \(\psi_i=\phi^i\), and the theoretical variance \(\sigma_a^2\sum\psi_i^2\) matches a simulation:

phi <- 0.6; psi <- phi^(0:200)
theo_var <- 1 * sum(psi^2)                          # sigma_a^2 = 1
set.seed(2); sim <- as.numeric(arima.sim(list(ar = phi), n = 1e5))
c(theoretical = theo_var, `closed form 1/(1-phi^2)` = 1/(1 - phi^2),
  simulated = var(sim))
            theoretical closed form 1/(1-phi^2)               simulated 
               1.562500                1.562500                1.570892

5 Autoregressive (AR) models

Slides §2.4. AR(1): \(r_t=\phi_0+\phi_1 r_{t-1}+a_t\), stationary iff \(|\phi_1|<1\), with \(\rho_\ell=\phi_1^\ell\). PACF cuts off at the order \(p\).

Theoretical ACFs (AR(1) and AR(2) via the Yule–Walker recursion):

ar2_acf <- function(p1, p2, n = 20) {
  r <- numeric(n + 1); r[1] <- 1; r[2] <- p1 / (1 - p2)
  for (k in 3:(n + 1)) r[k] <- p1 * r[k-1] + p2 * r[k-2]; r
}
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
barplot(0.8^(0:15),  names.arg = 0:15, col = "steelblue", border = NA,
        main = expression(AR(1)~phi==0.8), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot((-0.8)^(0:15), names.arg = 0:15, col = "tomato", border = NA,
        main = expression(AR(1)~phi==-0.8), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot(ar2_acf(0.6, -0.4), names.arg = 0:20, col = "darkgreen", border = NA,
        main = "AR(2) complex roots (cyclical)", ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
par(op)

Fit an AR to real data. Daily S&P 500 returns have genuine AR(1) structure. We compare the Yule–Walker estimate by hand to ar() and arima():

# Yule-Walker for AR(1): phi1_hat = rho1_hat
r1 <- samp_acf(sp_d, 1)[2]
yw_ar  <- ar(sp_d, order.max = 1, method = "yw",  aic = FALSE)
mle_ar <- arima(sp_d, order = c(1, 0, 0))
c(`hand (rho1)` = r1, `ar() yule-walker` = yw_ar$ar,
  `arima() css-ml` = as.numeric(coef(mle_ar)["ar1"]))
     hand (rho1) ar() yule-walker   arima() css-ml 
     -0.09912083      -0.09912083      -0.09926079

Order selection (AIC vs BIC). AIC is looser and reaches for a bigger model; BIC is parsimonious and consistent.

T_n <- length(sp_d)
ord <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:7, function(p) {
  f <- arima(sp_d, order = c(p, 0, 0))
  data.frame(p = p, AIC = AIC(f), BIC = AIC(f, k = log(T_n)))
}))
kable(ord, digits = 1, caption = "AR(p) on daily S&P 500 returns: AIC vs BIC.")
AR(p) on daily S&P 500 returns: AIC vs BIC.
p AIC BIC
1 -39769.5 -39749.1
2 -39768.7 -39741.5
3 -39766.8 -39732.7
4 -39769.1 -39728.3
5 -39768.7 -39721.1
6 -39774.7 -39720.3
7 -39775.4 -39714.2 
cat(sprintf("AIC picks p = %d ; BIC picks p = %d\n",
            ord$p[which.min(ord$AIC)], ord$p[which.min(ord$BIC)]))
AIC picks p = 7 ; BIC picks p = 1

PACF and diagnostics. The daily PACF shows one significant (negative) spike; the AR(1) residuals should be white noise.

op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
pacf(sp_d, lag.max = 15, main = "PACF: daily S&P 500 returns")
acf(residuals(mle_ar), lag.max = 20, main = "ACF of AR(1) residuals"); par(op)
# Residual Ljung-Box with df = m - p
m <- 12; Q <- ljung_box(residuals(mle_ar), m)$Q
data.frame(Q = Q, df = m - 1, p_value = 1 - pchisq(Q, m - 1))

Forecasting. AR forecasts revert to the mean; the intervals widen and stabilise at the unconditional variance.

pr <- predict(mle_ar, n.ahead = 20)
mu <- as.numeric(coef(mle_ar)["intercept"])
plot(1:20, 100 * pr$pred, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "tomato", ylim = c(-1.2, 1.2),
     xlab = "steps ahead", ylab = "forecast (%)",
     main = "AR(1) forecast of S&P 500 daily returns -> mu")
lines(1:20, 100 * (pr$pred + 1.96 * pr$se), lty = 2, col = "steelblue")
lines(1:20, 100 * (pr$pred - 1.96 * pr$se), lty = 2, col = "steelblue")
abline(h = 100 * mu, col = "grey40", lty = 3)

6 Moving-average (MA) models

Slides §2.5. MA(1): \(r_t=c_0+a_t-\theta_1 a_{t-1}\). Always stationary; ACF cuts off at lag \(q\); \(\rho_1=-\theta_1/(1+\theta_1^2)\) with \(|\rho_1|\le0.5\).

ma1_acf <- function(th) c(1, -th / (1 + th^2), rep(0, 12))
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
barplot(ma1_acf(0.5),  names.arg = 0:13, col = "steelblue", border = NA,
        main = expression(MA(1)~theta==0.5), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot(ma1_acf(-0.5), names.arg = 0:13, col = "tomato", border = NA,
        main = expression(MA(1)~theta==-0.5), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
par(op)

The MA(1) ceiling \(|\rho_1|\le0.5\) is worth seeing — it is reached only as \(|\theta|\to1\) (the invertibility boundary):

th <- seq(-3, 3, 0.01); rho1 <- -th / (1 + th^2)
plot(th, rho1, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(rho[1]),
     main = expression("MA(1): " ~ rho[1] == -theta/(1+theta^2) ~ ", bounded by 0.5"))
abline(h = c(-0.5, 0.5), lty = 2, col = "tomato")

Fitting an MA(1) to daily S&P returns recovers a negative \(\rho_1\) consistent with the bid–ask-bounce story (an MA(1) and an AR(1) both rationalise that one spike — diagnostics and parsimony decide):

ma1 <- arima(sp_d, order = c(0, 0, 1))
data.frame(theta1 = coef(ma1)["ma1"], implied_rho1 = samp_acf(sp_d,1)[2],
           resid_LB12_p = ljung_box(residuals(ma1), 12)$p_value)

7 ARMA models

Slides §2.6. ARMA mixes AR and MA for parsimony; both ACF and PACF tail off. Three representations: ARMA form, MA(\(\infty\)) (\(\psi\)-weights), AR(\(\infty\)) (\(\pi\)-weights).

Both functions tail off — the fingerprint of a mixed model:

set.seed(3)
x <- arima.sim(list(ar = 0.7, ma = -0.4), n = 4000)
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
acf(x, lag.max = 15, main = "ARMA(1,1) ACF: tails off")
pacf(x, lag.max = 15, main = "ARMA(1,1) PACF: also tails off"); par(op)

Three representations of one ARMA(1,1) (\(\phi=0.7,\theta=-0.4\)). The \(\psi\)- and \(\pi\)-weights are obtained directly with ARMAtoMA/ARMAtoAR:

ar_c <- 0.7; ma_c <- -0.4
# MA(inf): psi(B) = theta(B)/phi(B)  -- impulse response
psi <- ARMAtoMA(ar = ar_c, ma = ma_c, lag.max = 6)
# AR(inf): pi(B) = phi(B)/theta(B). Base R has no ARMAtoAR, but pi-weights are
# the MA(inf) expansion with the AR and MA polynomials swapped (and negated).
pii <- ARMAtoMA(ar = -ma_c, ma = -ar_c, lag.max = 6)
kable(data.frame(lag = 1:6, psi_MAinf = psi, pi_ARinf = pii), digits = 4,
      caption = "psi-weights (impulse response) and pi-weights (forecast weights).")
psi-weights (impulse response) and pi-weights (forecast weights).
lag psi_MAinf pi_ARinf
1 0.3000 -0.3000
2 0.2100 -0.1200
3 0.1470 -0.0480
4 0.1029 -0.0192
5 0.0720 -0.0077
6 0.0504 -0.0031

Identification on real data. Use EACF if TSA is installed, otherwise auto.arima, otherwise a small BIC grid we code by hand:

if (pk["TSA"]) {
  TSA::eacf(sp_d, ar.max = 5, ma.max = 5)
} else if (pk["forecast"]) {
  print(forecast::auto.arima(sp_d, max.p = 5, max.q = 5, seasonal = FALSE,
                             stationary = TRUE, ic = "bic", stepwise = FALSE))
} else {
  grid <- expand.grid(p = 0:3, q = 0:3)
  grid$BIC <- mapply(function(p, q)
    tryCatch(AIC(arima(sp_d, c(p,0,q)), k = log(T_n)), error = function(e) NA),
    grid$p, grid$q)
  kable(grid[order(grid$BIC), ][1:5, ], digits = 1,
        caption = "Top-5 ARMA(p,q) for daily S&P 500 by BIC (hand grid).")
}
Series: sp_d 
ARIMA(1,0,0) with zero mean 

Coefficients:
          ar1
      -0.0988
s.e.   0.0122

sigma^2 = 0.0001466:  log likelihood = 19886.04
AIC=-39768.07   AICc=-39768.07   BIC=-39754.47

8 Unit-root nonstationarity

Slides §2.7. A random walk is an AR(1) with \(\phi=1\): variance grows with \(t\), shocks are permanent. Prices have a unit root; first differences (returns) do not. The ADF test has \(H_0\): unit root.

The ADF regression, by hand. Regress \(\Delta p_t\) on \(p_{t-1}\) and \(k\) lagged differences; the \(t\)-ratio on \(p_{t-1}\) is the ADF statistic (compare to Dickey–Fuller critical values, not the normal table).

adf_t <- function(y, k = 10, drift = TRUE) {
  y  <- y[is.finite(y)]; dy <- diff(y); n <- length(dy)
  Y  <- dy[(k+1):n]
  L1 <- y[(k+1):n]                                  # p_{t-1}
  X  <- sapply(1:k, function(i) dy[(k+1-i):(n-i)])  # lagged differences
  X  <- cbind(L1, X); if (drift) X <- cbind(1, X)
  fit <- lm.fit(X, Y)
  b   <- fit$coefficients; r <- fit$residuals
  s2  <- sum(r^2) / (length(Y) - ncol(X))
  XtX <- solve(crossprod(X)); se <- sqrt(s2 * diag(XtX))
  j <- if (drift) 2 else 1                          # position of L1 coefficient
  unname(b[j] / se[j])
}
lp <- log(sp_px$Price[is.finite(sp_px$Price)])
data.frame(
  series      = c("log price", "log return"),
  ADF_t       = c(adf_t(lp, drift = TRUE), adf_t(sp_d, drift = FALSE)),
  crit_5pct   = c(-2.86, -1.94),
  decision    = c("fail to reject H0 (unit root)", "reject H0 (stationary)"))
if (pk["urca"]) {
  cat("Cross-check with urca::ur.df --\n")
  print(urca::ur.df(lp,   type = "drift", lags = 10, selectlags = "AIC")@teststat)
  print(urca::ur.df(sp_d, type = "none",  lags = 10, selectlags = "AIC")@teststat)
}
Cross-check with urca::ur.df --
              tau2     phi1
statistic 1.055561 2.437372
               tau1
statistic -27.71206

Random walk vs stationary (simulation). Variance of a random walk grows linearly in \(t\) — the visual signature of non-stationarity.

set.seed(42); n <- 252
rw <- cumsum(rnorm(n)); rwd <- cumsum(rnorm(n, 0.3)); wn <- rnorm(n)
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(rw,  type = "l", col = "steelblue", main = "Random walk", xlab = "t")
plot(rwd, type = "l", col = "darkgreen", main = "RW + drift", xlab = "t")
plot(wn,  type = "l", col = "tomato",   main = "i.i.d. noise", xlab = "t"); abline(h = 0)
par(op)
cat(sprintf("Var of first vs second half of RW: %.2f then %.2f (grows)\n",
            var(rw[1:126]), var(rw[127:252])))
Var of first vs second half of RW: 7.32 then 4.39 (grows)

9 Seasonal models

Slides §2.8. Seasonal differencing \((1-B^s)\); multiplicative SARIMA \((p,d,q)\times(P,D,Q)_s\). Equity returns are not seasonal, so we demonstrate the method on a labelled simulated series and verify the absence of seasonality in real returns.

set.seed(7); N <- 180
season <- rep(c(.1,.05,-.05,-.1,-.05,0,.05,.1,.05,0,-.05,-.1), N/12)
y <- ts(seq(0, .5, length.out = N) + season + rnorm(N, 0, .03),
        frequency = 12)
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(y, main = "(a) SIMULATED seasonal series (s=12)")
acf(as.numeric(y), lag.max = 36, main = "(b) ACF: spikes at 12, 24, 36")
acf(sp_m, lag.max = 36, main = "(c) REAL S&P monthly returns: no spikes")
plot(diff(diff(y, 12)), main = "(d) After seasonal+regular difference")
par(op)
# the real seasonal-lag autocorrelations are tiny
data.frame(lag = c(12, 24, 36), acf = samp_acf(sp_m, 36)[c(13, 25, 37)])

If forecast is available, auto.arima recovers the airline-type structure on the simulated series:

if (pk["forecast"])
  print(forecast::auto.arima(y, seasonal = TRUE, stepwise = FALSE, ic = "bic"))
Series: y 
ARIMA(0,0,0)(2,1,0)[12] with drift 

Coefficients:
         sar1     sar2   drift
      -0.5837  -0.3807  0.0027
s.e.   0.0785   0.0770  0.0001

sigma^2 = 0.001027:  log likelihood = 338.07
AIC=-668.14   AICc=-667.89   BIC=-655.64

10 Regression with time series errors

Slides §2.9. When regression errors are serially correlated, OLS coefficients stay consistent but the standard errors are wrong (usually too small), so \(t\)-tests mislead.

We regress the DAX level on the S&P 500 level — two near-unit-root series. The slope looks wildly significant under OLS, but the residuals are massively autocorrelated.

df  <- na.omit(data.frame(SP500 = indices$SP500, DAX = indices$DAX))
ols <- lm(DAX ~ SP500, data = df)
res <- residuals(ols)
cat(sprintf("beta = %.3f   OLS se = %.4f   OLS t = %.0f   R^2 = %.3f\n",
            coef(ols)[2], summary(ols)$coefficients[2,2],
            summary(ols)$coefficients[2,3], summary(ols)$r.squared))
beta = 3.241   OLS se = 0.0097   OLS t = 334   R^2 = 0.945
cat(sprintf("Residual lag-1 ACF = %.3f   Ljung-Box(20) p = %.3g\n",
            samp_acf(res,1)[2], ljung_box(res, 20)$p_value))
Residual lag-1 ACF = 0.995   Ljung-Box(20) p = 0
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(res, type = "l", col = "steelblue", main = "OLS residuals: long slow swings",
     xlab = "obs", ylab = "residual"); abline(h = 0, col = "grey40")
acf(res, lag.max = 30, main = "ACF of residuals: strongly autocorrelated"); par(op)

11 Consistent covariance estimation (HAC)

Slides §2.10. The Newey–West / HAC estimator replaces the OLS “meat” with a Bartlett-weighted sum of residual autocovariances, restoring valid standard errors under heteroskedasticity and autocorrelation.

We build the HAC standard error by hand and confirm it against sandwich::NeweyWest. The honest SE is several times the OLS one — the OLS \(t\)-statistic was an illusion.

hac_se <- function(model, L) {
  X <- model.matrix(model); u <- residuals(model); n <- nrow(X)
  bread <- solve(crossprod(X))
  meat  <- crossprod(X * u)                         # Gamma_0
  for (j in 1:L) {                                  # Bartlett-weighted lags
    w  <- 1 - j / (L + 1)
    Xu <- X * u
    G  <- crossprod(Xu[(j+1):n, , drop = FALSE], Xu[1:(n-j), , drop = FALSE])
    meat <- meat + w * (G + t(G))
  }
  sqrt(diag(bread %*% meat %*% bread))
}
L <- 20
hand <- hac_se(ols, L)["SP500"]
out  <- c(OLS_se = summary(ols)$coefficients["SP500","Std. Error"],
          HAC_hand = unname(hand))
if (pk["sandwich"]) out["HAC_NeweyWest"] <- sqrt(sandwich::NeweyWest(ols, lag = L)["SP500","SP500"])
out["ratio_HAC_over_OLS"] <- out["HAC_hand"] / out["OLS_se"]
kable(t(round(out, 5)), caption = "Standard error of the slope: OLS vs HAC.")
Standard error of the slope: OLS vs HAC.
OLS_se HAC_hand ratio_HAC_over_OLS
0.0097 0.04368 4.50224 
if (pk["lmtest"] && pk["sandwich"])
  lmtest::coeftest(ols, vcov = sandwich::NeweyWest(ols, lag = L))

12 Long-memory models

Slides §2.11. Fractional differencing \((1-B)^d x_t=a_t\), \(0<d<0.5\), gives a stationary series whose ACF decays hyperbolically. Returns are short memory (\(d\approx0\)); their volatility proxies are long memory.

The contrast is stark: \(r_t\) decorrelates immediately, but \(|r_t|\) and \(r_t^2\) stay autocorrelated for hundreds of lags.

L <- 100
ar_abs <- samp_acf(abs(sp_d), L); ar_sq <- samp_acf(sp_d^2, L); ar_r <- samp_acf(sp_d, L)
plot(0:L, ar_abs, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2, ylim = c(-.05, .4),
     xlab = "lag (days)", ylab = "ACF", main = "S&P 500: returns vs |returns| vs squared")
lines(0:L, ar_sq, col = "tomato", lwd = 2); lines(0:L, ar_r, col = "grey50")
abline(h = 0); legend("topright", c("|r|","r^2","r"),
       col = c("steelblue","tomato","grey50"), lwd = 2, bty = "n")

GPH estimator of \(d\), by hand (log-periodogram regression), cross-checked against fracdiff if installed:

gph_d <- function(x, m = floor(length(x)^0.5)) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; n <- length(x)
  I <- (Mod(fft(x - mean(x)))^2) / (2 * pi * n)     # periodogram
  j <- 1:m; lam <- 2 * pi * j / n
  reg <- lm(log(I[j + 1]) ~ I(-2 * log(2 * sin(lam / 2))))
  unname(coef(reg)[2])                              # slope estimates d
}
d_tab <- data.frame(series = c("|r|", "r^2", "r"),
                    GPH_d  = c(gph_d(abs(sp_d)), gph_d(sp_d^2), gph_d(sp_d)))
kable(d_tab, digits = 3, caption = "GPH long-memory estimates (d>0 => long memory).")
GPH long-memory estimates (d>0 => long memory).
series GPH_d
|r| 0.429
r^2 0.298
r 0.001 
if (pk["fracdiff"]) {
  fd <- fracdiff::fracdiff(abs(sp_d), nar = 0, nma = 0)
  cat(sprintf("fracdiff d for |r| = %.3f (GPH gave %.3f)\n", fd$d, gph_d(abs(sp_d))))
}
fracdiff d for |r| = 0.230 (GPH gave 0.429)

d ≈ 0.4 for |r| versus d ≈ 0 for r: volatility has long memory even though direction does not. This is precisely the empirical fact that motivates the ARCH/GARCH machinery of Chapter 3.

13 Key takeaways

  1. Stationarity is the licence to do inference. Returns qualify; prices do not. One difference bridges the two.
  2. ACF/PACF classify; Ljung–Box tests. AR ⇒ PACF cuts off; MA ⇒ ACF cuts off; ARMA ⇒ both tail off.
  3. Frequency matters. Daily index returns carry a small negative AR(1); monthly returns are white noise. Don’t over-model.
  4. Build, then check. Use AIC/BIC and EACF for order, but the verdict is always white-noise residuals — not a big \(R^2\).
  5. Unit roots demand differencing, not detrending; the ADF test has a non-standard distribution.
  6. Serially correlated regression errors break OLS standard errors — fix with HAC (Newey–West) or by modelling the error process.
  7. Returns are short memory; volatility is long memory. That single asymmetry is the doorway to Chapter 3 (ARCH/GARCH).

Reproduce everything with quarto render FI362_Ch2_Supplement.qmd. Change the running series by swapping a column name — the code is column-agnostic.

Source Code
---
title: "FI 362 — Chapter 2 Supplement"
subtitle: "Linear Time Series Analysis, Step by Step on Real Data"
author: "Mehman Ismayilli"
date: last-modified
date-format: long
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 7
    fig-height: 4
    fig-align: center
    embed-resources: true
    df-print: paged
engine: knitr
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

> **How to use this notebook.** It mirrors the Chapter 2 slides
> (`FI362_Ch2`) section by section, but *computes* every concept on real market
> data instead of stating it abstractly. Read the slide, then run the matching
> block here. All data lives in the sibling `data/` folder built by
> `data/download_data.R` (daily Yahoo Finance prices, Jan 2000 – mid 2026,
> adjusted for splits & dividends). **No internet is needed to run this file.**
>
> Where it adds value, we *hand-code* the core formula (sample ACF, Ljung–Box,
> Yule–Walker, the ADF regression, the GPH long-memory estimator) and then
> cross-check it against the standard package. Heavier packages (`forecast`,
> `urca`, `fracdiff`, `TSA`) are used only when installed — each block degrades
> gracefully.

# Setup & data

```{r setup}
library(knitr)

# Optional packages: we use them if present, but never require them.
have <- function(p) requireNamespace(p, quietly = TRUE)
pk <- c(forecast = have("forecast"), urca = have("urca"),
        fracdiff = have("fracdiff"), TSA = have("TSA"),
        sandwich = have("sandwich"), lmtest = have("lmtest"))
kable(data.frame(package = names(pk), installed = unname(pk)),
      caption = "Optional packages detected on this machine.")

# Locate data/ robustly (works from this folder, the project root, or RStudio).
data_dir <- local({
  cands <- c("../data", "data", file.path(getwd(), "data"),
             file.path(dirname(getwd()), "data"))
  hit <- cands[file.exists(file.path(cands, "indices_adjclose.csv"))]
  if (!length(hit)) stop("Could not find data/. Run data/download_data.R first.")
  hit[1]
})

read_prices <- function(file) {
  df <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, file), check.names = FALSE)
  df$Date <- as.Date(df$Date); df
}

indices   <- read_prices("indices_adjclose.csv")   # daily, wide (11 indices)
stocks    <- read_prices("stocks_adjclose.csv")    # daily, wide (5 US stocks)
idx_month <- read_prices("indices_monthly.csv")    # month-end, wide
sp_month  <- read_prices("sp500_monthly.csv")      # month-end S&P 500
meta      <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "metadata.csv"))

kable(meta[, c("symbol","name","region","asset_class","rows","start","end")],
      caption = "The universe of global indices and US stocks.")
```

```{r helpers}
# --- returns -----------------------------------------------------------------
log_ret <- function(p) { p <- p[is.finite(p)]; diff(log(p)) }   # r_t = ln P_t - ln P_{t-1}

# month-end series from a daily (Date, price) frame
to_monthly <- function(date, price) {
  ok <- is.finite(price); date <- date[ok]; price <- price[ok]
  key <- format(date, "%Y-%m")
  idx <- tapply(seq_along(price), key, max)        # last obs in each month
  data.frame(Date = date[idx], Price = price[idx])
}

# --- sample ACF EXACTLY as on the slides -------------------------------------
samp_acf <- function(x, lag.max) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x); xb <- mean(x)
  g0 <- sum((x - xb)^2)
  sapply(0:lag.max, function(l)
    sum((x[(l+1):T] - xb) * (x[1:(T-l)] - xb)) / g0)
}

# --- Ljung–Box Q(m) by hand --------------------------------------------------
ljung_box <- function(x, m) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x)
  r <- samp_acf(x, m)[-1]                          # drop lag 0
  Q <- T * (T + 2) * sum(r^2 / (T - (1:m)))
  data.frame(m = m, Q = Q, df = m, p_value = 1 - pchisq(Q, m))
}

# running example: S&P 500 (index) and AAPL (stock)
# keep only rows with a finite S&P price so dates and returns stay aligned
# (the 11 global indices share one calendar, so SP500 has gaps on non-US sessions)
.ok_sp <- is.finite(indices$SP500)
sp_px  <- data.frame(Date = indices$Date[.ok_sp], Price = indices$SP500[.ok_sp])
sp_d   <- log_ret(sp_px$Price)                     # daily S&P 500 log returns
sp_m   <- log_ret(sp_month$SP500)                  # monthly S&P 500 log returns
aapl_d <- log_ret(stocks$AAPL)                     # daily AAPL log returns
aapl_m <- log_ret(to_monthly(stocks$Date, stocks$AAPL)$Price)
```

We use the **S&P 500** as the running index and **AAPL** as the running stock,
but every computation is column-agnostic — swap `"SP500"` for `"DAX"`, `"AAPL"`
for `"MSFT"`, etc.

# Stationarity {#sec-stationarity}

> *Slides §2.1.* Weak stationarity = constant mean **and** an autocovariance that
> depends only on the lag. A stationary series fluctuates around a fixed level
> with constant spread; a non-stationary one wanders.

The cleanest demonstration is the same asset one difference apart: the **price**
wanders (non-stationary), the **return** is anchored at zero (stationary).

```{r stationarity-plot, fig.height=5}
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2, 1))
plot(sp_px$Date, log(sp_px$Price), type = "l", col = "tomato",
     xlab = "", ylab = "log price", main = "S&P 500 log price — wandering level (non-stationary)")
plot(sp_px$Date[-1], 100 * sp_d, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "", ylab = "log return (%)", main = "S&P 500 log return — fixed level, ~constant spread (stationary)")
abline(h = 0, col = "grey40"); par(op)
```

A quick numerical check: split the return series in half and compare moments.
For a stationary series the two halves should look alike; for the price they do
not.

```{r stationarity-moments}
half <- function(x) { x <- x[is.finite(x)]; n <- length(x)
  data.frame(first_mean = mean(x[1:(n%/%2)]),  second_mean = mean(x[(n%/%2+1):n]),
             first_sd   = sd(x[1:(n%/%2)]),    second_sd   = sd(x[(n%/%2+1):n])) }
kable(rbind(`log price` = half(log(sp_px$Price)),
            `log return` = half(sp_d)),
      digits = 4, caption = "Mean/SD across the two halves of the sample.")
```

# Correlation and the ACF {#sec-acf}

> *Slides §2.2.* $\rho_\ell=\gamma_\ell/\gamma_0$. Under i.i.d.,
> $\hat\rho_\ell\sim N(0,1/T)$, so bands are $\pm1.96/\sqrt T$. The Ljung–Box
> statistic $Q(m)=T(T+2)\sum_{\ell=1}^m\hat\rho_\ell^2/(T-\ell)\to\chi^2_m$.

**Hand-coded vs built-in ACF** — they agree to machine precision:

```{r acf-check}
hand <- samp_acf(sp_d, 10)[-1]
base <- as.numeric(acf(sp_d, lag.max = 10, plot = FALSE)$acf)[-1]
kable(data.frame(lag = 1:10, hand_coded = hand, base_acf = base,
                 diff = hand - base), digits = 6,
      caption = "Sample ACF of daily S&P 500 returns: our formula vs acf().")
```

**Frequency changes the verdict.** Daily index returns carry a clear negative
lag-1 autocorrelation (bid–ask bounce / micro mean-reversion); monthly returns
are indistinguishable from white noise.

```{r acf-plots, fig.height=5}
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
acf(sp_d,   lag.max = 30, main = "Daily S&P 500 returns")
acf(aapl_d, lag.max = 30, main = "Daily AAPL returns")
acf(sp_m,   lag.max = 24, main = "Monthly S&P 500 returns")
acf(aapl_m, lag.max = 24, main = "Monthly AAPL returns"); par(op)
```

**Ljung–Box, hand-coded vs `Box.test`:**

```{r ljungbox}
lb <- rbind(
  cbind(series = "S&P daily",   ljung_box(sp_d, 10)),
  cbind(series = "S&P monthly", ljung_box(sp_m, 5)),
  cbind(series = "S&P monthly", ljung_box(sp_m, 12)),
  cbind(series = "AAPL daily",  ljung_box(aapl_d, 10)))
kable(lb, digits = 4, caption = "Ljung–Box (hand-coded).")

# cross-check one of them against the base function
c(hand = ljung_box(sp_d, 10)$p_value,
  base = Box.test(sp_d, lag = 10, type = "Ljung-Box")$p.value)
```

Read-off: daily returns reject "no serial correlation" decisively
($p<10^{-15}$); monthly returns do **not** ($p\approx0.20$). Markets are closer
to efficient at the monthly horizon.

# White noise & linear time series {#sec-wn}

> *Slides §2.3.* White noise $\{a_t\}$ is i.i.d. with mean 0, finite variance,
> all ACFs zero. A *linear* series is $r_t=\mu+\sum_{i\ge0}\psi_i a_{t-i}$ with
> $\psi_0=1$; the $\psi$-weights are the impulse response and give the variance,
> ACF, and forecast-error variance.

```{r wn-demo, fig.height=3.4}
set.seed(1)
a <- rnorm(500)                                    # Gaussian white noise
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(a, type = "l", col = "grey40", main = "White noise", xlab = "t", ylab = expression(a[t]))
acf(a, lag.max = 20, main = "ACF ~ 0 at all lags"); par(op)
```

The $\psi$-weights make AR/MA/ARMA a single family. For an AR(1) with $\phi=0.6$,
$\psi_i=\phi^i$, and the theoretical variance $\sigma_a^2\sum\psi_i^2$ matches a
simulation:

```{r psi-weights}
phi <- 0.6; psi <- phi^(0:200)
theo_var <- 1 * sum(psi^2)                          # sigma_a^2 = 1
set.seed(2); sim <- as.numeric(arima.sim(list(ar = phi), n = 1e5))
c(theoretical = theo_var, `closed form 1/(1-phi^2)` = 1/(1 - phi^2),
  simulated = var(sim))
```

# Autoregressive (AR) models {#sec-ar}

> *Slides §2.4.* AR(1): $r_t=\phi_0+\phi_1 r_{t-1}+a_t$, stationary iff
> $|\phi_1|<1$, with $\rho_\ell=\phi_1^\ell$. PACF cuts off at the order $p$.

**Theoretical ACFs** (AR(1) and AR(2) via the Yule–Walker recursion):

```{r ar-theory, fig.height=3.6}
ar2_acf <- function(p1, p2, n = 20) {
  r <- numeric(n + 1); r[1] <- 1; r[2] <- p1 / (1 - p2)
  for (k in 3:(n + 1)) r[k] <- p1 * r[k-1] + p2 * r[k-2]; r
}
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
barplot(0.8^(0:15),  names.arg = 0:15, col = "steelblue", border = NA,
        main = expression(AR(1)~phi==0.8), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot((-0.8)^(0:15), names.arg = 0:15, col = "tomato", border = NA,
        main = expression(AR(1)~phi==-0.8), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot(ar2_acf(0.6, -0.4), names.arg = 0:20, col = "darkgreen", border = NA,
        main = "AR(2) complex roots (cyclical)", ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
par(op)
```

**Fit an AR to real data.** Daily S&P 500 returns have genuine AR(1) structure.
We compare the **Yule–Walker** estimate by hand to `ar()` and `arima()`:

```{r ar-fit}
# Yule-Walker for AR(1): phi1_hat = rho1_hat
r1 <- samp_acf(sp_d, 1)[2]
yw_ar  <- ar(sp_d, order.max = 1, method = "yw",  aic = FALSE)
mle_ar <- arima(sp_d, order = c(1, 0, 0))
c(`hand (rho1)` = r1, `ar() yule-walker` = yw_ar$ar,
  `arima() css-ml` = as.numeric(coef(mle_ar)["ar1"]))
```

**Order selection (AIC vs BIC).** AIC is looser and reaches for a bigger model;
BIC is parsimonious and consistent.

```{r ar-order}
T_n <- length(sp_d)
ord <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:7, function(p) {
  f <- arima(sp_d, order = c(p, 0, 0))
  data.frame(p = p, AIC = AIC(f), BIC = AIC(f, k = log(T_n)))
}))
kable(ord, digits = 1, caption = "AR(p) on daily S&P 500 returns: AIC vs BIC.")
cat(sprintf("AIC picks p = %d ; BIC picks p = %d\n",
            ord$p[which.min(ord$AIC)], ord$p[which.min(ord$BIC)]))
```

**PACF and diagnostics.** The daily PACF shows one significant (negative) spike;
the AR(1) residuals should be white noise.

```{r ar-diag, fig.height=3.4}
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
pacf(sp_d, lag.max = 15, main = "PACF: daily S&P 500 returns")
acf(residuals(mle_ar), lag.max = 20, main = "ACF of AR(1) residuals"); par(op)
# Residual Ljung-Box with df = m - p
m <- 12; Q <- ljung_box(residuals(mle_ar), m)$Q
data.frame(Q = Q, df = m - 1, p_value = 1 - pchisq(Q, m - 1))
```

**Forecasting.** AR forecasts revert to the mean; the intervals widen and
stabilise at the unconditional variance.

```{r ar-forecast, fig.height=3.6}
pr <- predict(mle_ar, n.ahead = 20)
mu <- as.numeric(coef(mle_ar)["intercept"])
plot(1:20, 100 * pr$pred, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "tomato", ylim = c(-1.2, 1.2),
     xlab = "steps ahead", ylab = "forecast (%)",
     main = "AR(1) forecast of S&P 500 daily returns -> mu")
lines(1:20, 100 * (pr$pred + 1.96 * pr$se), lty = 2, col = "steelblue")
lines(1:20, 100 * (pr$pred - 1.96 * pr$se), lty = 2, col = "steelblue")
abline(h = 100 * mu, col = "grey40", lty = 3)
```

# Moving-average (MA) models {#sec-ma}

> *Slides §2.5.* MA(1): $r_t=c_0+a_t-\theta_1 a_{t-1}$. Always stationary;
> ACF cuts off at lag $q$; $\rho_1=-\theta_1/(1+\theta_1^2)$ with $|\rho_1|\le0.5$.

```{r ma-theory, fig.height=3.4}
ma1_acf <- function(th) c(1, -th / (1 + th^2), rep(0, 12))
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
barplot(ma1_acf(0.5),  names.arg = 0:13, col = "steelblue", border = NA,
        main = expression(MA(1)~theta==0.5), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
barplot(ma1_acf(-0.5), names.arg = 0:13, col = "tomato", border = NA,
        main = expression(MA(1)~theta==-0.5), ylim = c(-1,1)); abline(h = 0)
par(op)
```

The MA(1) ceiling $|\rho_1|\le0.5$ is worth seeing — it is reached only as
$|\theta|\to1$ (the invertibility boundary):

```{r ma-ceiling}
th <- seq(-3, 3, 0.01); rho1 <- -th / (1 + th^2)
plot(th, rho1, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(rho[1]),
     main = expression("MA(1): " ~ rho[1] == -theta/(1+theta^2) ~ ", bounded by 0.5"))
abline(h = c(-0.5, 0.5), lty = 2, col = "tomato")
```

Fitting an MA(1) to daily S&P returns recovers a negative $\rho_1$ consistent
with the bid–ask-bounce story (an MA(1) and an AR(1) both rationalise that one
spike — diagnostics and parsimony decide):

```{r ma-fit}
ma1 <- arima(sp_d, order = c(0, 0, 1))
data.frame(theta1 = coef(ma1)["ma1"], implied_rho1 = samp_acf(sp_d,1)[2],
           resid_LB12_p = ljung_box(residuals(ma1), 12)$p_value)
```

# ARMA models {#sec-arma}

> *Slides §2.6.* ARMA mixes AR and MA for parsimony; both ACF and PACF tail off.
> Three representations: ARMA form, MA($\infty$) ($\psi$-weights), AR($\infty$)
> ($\pi$-weights).

**Both functions tail off** — the fingerprint of a mixed model:

```{r arma-acf, fig.height=3.4}
set.seed(3)
x <- arima.sim(list(ar = 0.7, ma = -0.4), n = 4000)
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
acf(x, lag.max = 15, main = "ARMA(1,1) ACF: tails off")
pacf(x, lag.max = 15, main = "ARMA(1,1) PACF: also tails off"); par(op)
```

**Three representations of one ARMA(1,1)** ($\phi=0.7,\theta=-0.4$). The
$\psi$- and $\pi$-weights are obtained directly with `ARMAtoMA`/`ARMAtoAR`:

```{r arma-reps}
ar_c <- 0.7; ma_c <- -0.4
# MA(inf): psi(B) = theta(B)/phi(B)  -- impulse response
psi <- ARMAtoMA(ar = ar_c, ma = ma_c, lag.max = 6)
# AR(inf): pi(B) = phi(B)/theta(B). Base R has no ARMAtoAR, but pi-weights are
# the MA(inf) expansion with the AR and MA polynomials swapped (and negated).
pii <- ARMAtoMA(ar = -ma_c, ma = -ar_c, lag.max = 6)
kable(data.frame(lag = 1:6, psi_MAinf = psi, pi_ARinf = pii), digits = 4,
      caption = "psi-weights (impulse response) and pi-weights (forecast weights).")
```

**Identification on real data.** Use EACF if `TSA` is installed, otherwise
`auto.arima`, otherwise a small BIC grid we code by hand:

```{r arma-id}
if (pk["TSA"]) {
  TSA::eacf(sp_d, ar.max = 5, ma.max = 5)
} else if (pk["forecast"]) {
  print(forecast::auto.arima(sp_d, max.p = 5, max.q = 5, seasonal = FALSE,
                             stationary = TRUE, ic = "bic", stepwise = FALSE))
} else {
  grid <- expand.grid(p = 0:3, q = 0:3)
  grid$BIC <- mapply(function(p, q)
    tryCatch(AIC(arima(sp_d, c(p,0,q)), k = log(T_n)), error = function(e) NA),
    grid$p, grid$q)
  kable(grid[order(grid$BIC), ][1:5, ], digits = 1,
        caption = "Top-5 ARMA(p,q) for daily S&P 500 by BIC (hand grid).")
}
```

# Unit-root nonstationarity {#sec-unitroot}

> *Slides §2.7.* A random walk is an AR(1) with $\phi=1$: variance grows with $t$,
> shocks are permanent. Prices have a unit root; first differences (returns) do
> not. The ADF test has $H_0$: unit root.

**The ADF regression, by hand.** Regress $\Delta p_t$ on $p_{t-1}$ and $k$
lagged differences; the $t$-ratio on $p_{t-1}$ is the ADF statistic (compare to
Dickey–Fuller critical values, **not** the normal table).

```{r adf-hand}
adf_t <- function(y, k = 10, drift = TRUE) {
  y  <- y[is.finite(y)]; dy <- diff(y); n <- length(dy)
  Y  <- dy[(k+1):n]
  L1 <- y[(k+1):n]                                  # p_{t-1}
  X  <- sapply(1:k, function(i) dy[(k+1-i):(n-i)])  # lagged differences
  X  <- cbind(L1, X); if (drift) X <- cbind(1, X)
  fit <- lm.fit(X, Y)
  b   <- fit$coefficients; r <- fit$residuals
  s2  <- sum(r^2) / (length(Y) - ncol(X))
  XtX <- solve(crossprod(X)); se <- sqrt(s2 * diag(XtX))
  j <- if (drift) 2 else 1                          # position of L1 coefficient
  unname(b[j] / se[j])
}
lp <- log(sp_px$Price[is.finite(sp_px$Price)])
data.frame(
  series      = c("log price", "log return"),
  ADF_t       = c(adf_t(lp, drift = TRUE), adf_t(sp_d, drift = FALSE)),
  crit_5pct   = c(-2.86, -1.94),
  decision    = c("fail to reject H0 (unit root)", "reject H0 (stationary)"))
```

```{r adf-urca}
if (pk["urca"]) {
  cat("Cross-check with urca::ur.df --\n")
  print(urca::ur.df(lp,   type = "drift", lags = 10, selectlags = "AIC")@teststat)
  print(urca::ur.df(sp_d, type = "none",  lags = 10, selectlags = "AIC")@teststat)
}
```

**Random walk vs stationary (simulation).** Variance of a random walk grows
linearly in $t$ — the visual signature of non-stationarity.

```{r rw-sim, fig.height=3.4}
set.seed(42); n <- 252
rw <- cumsum(rnorm(n)); rwd <- cumsum(rnorm(n, 0.3)); wn <- rnorm(n)
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(rw,  type = "l", col = "steelblue", main = "Random walk", xlab = "t")
plot(rwd, type = "l", col = "darkgreen", main = "RW + drift", xlab = "t")
plot(wn,  type = "l", col = "tomato",   main = "i.i.d. noise", xlab = "t"); abline(h = 0)
par(op)
cat(sprintf("Var of first vs second half of RW: %.2f then %.2f (grows)\n",
            var(rw[1:126]), var(rw[127:252])))
```

# Seasonal models {#sec-seasonal}

> *Slides §2.8.* Seasonal differencing $(1-B^s)$; multiplicative
> SARIMA $(p,d,q)\times(P,D,Q)_s$. Equity returns are **not** seasonal, so we
> demonstrate the *method* on a labelled simulated series and *verify* the
> absence of seasonality in real returns.

```{r seasonal, fig.height=5}
set.seed(7); N <- 180
season <- rep(c(.1,.05,-.05,-.1,-.05,0,.05,.1,.05,0,-.05,-.1), N/12)
y <- ts(seq(0, .5, length.out = N) + season + rnorm(N, 0, .03),
        frequency = 12)
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(y, main = "(a) SIMULATED seasonal series (s=12)")
acf(as.numeric(y), lag.max = 36, main = "(b) ACF: spikes at 12, 24, 36")
acf(sp_m, lag.max = 36, main = "(c) REAL S&P monthly returns: no spikes")
plot(diff(diff(y, 12)), main = "(d) After seasonal+regular difference")
par(op)
```

```{r seasonal-check}
# the real seasonal-lag autocorrelations are tiny
data.frame(lag = c(12, 24, 36), acf = samp_acf(sp_m, 36)[c(13, 25, 37)])
```

If `forecast` is available, `auto.arima` recovers the airline-type structure on
the simulated series:

```{r sarima}
if (pk["forecast"])
  print(forecast::auto.arima(y, seasonal = TRUE, stepwise = FALSE, ic = "bic"))
```

# Regression with time series errors {#sec-reg}

> *Slides §2.9.* When regression errors are serially correlated, OLS
> coefficients stay consistent but the standard errors are wrong (usually too
> small), so $t$-tests mislead.

We regress the **DAX level** on the **S&P 500 level** — two near-unit-root
series. The slope looks wildly significant under OLS, but the residuals are
massively autocorrelated.

```{r reg-ols}
df  <- na.omit(data.frame(SP500 = indices$SP500, DAX = indices$DAX))
ols <- lm(DAX ~ SP500, data = df)
res <- residuals(ols)
cat(sprintf("beta = %.3f   OLS se = %.4f   OLS t = %.0f   R^2 = %.3f\n",
            coef(ols)[2], summary(ols)$coefficients[2,2],
            summary(ols)$coefficients[2,3], summary(ols)$r.squared))
cat(sprintf("Residual lag-1 ACF = %.3f   Ljung-Box(20) p = %.3g\n",
            samp_acf(res,1)[2], ljung_box(res, 20)$p_value))
```

```{r reg-plot, fig.height=3.4}
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))
plot(res, type = "l", col = "steelblue", main = "OLS residuals: long slow swings",
     xlab = "obs", ylab = "residual"); abline(h = 0, col = "grey40")
acf(res, lag.max = 30, main = "ACF of residuals: strongly autocorrelated"); par(op)
```

# Consistent covariance estimation (HAC) {#sec-hac}

> *Slides §2.10.* The Newey–West / HAC estimator replaces the OLS "meat" with a
> Bartlett-weighted sum of residual autocovariances, restoring valid standard
> errors under heteroskedasticity **and** autocorrelation.

We build the HAC standard error **by hand** and confirm it against
`sandwich::NeweyWest`. The honest SE is several times the OLS one — the OLS
$t$-statistic was an illusion.

```{r hac-hand}
hac_se <- function(model, L) {
  X <- model.matrix(model); u <- residuals(model); n <- nrow(X)
  bread <- solve(crossprod(X))
  meat  <- crossprod(X * u)                         # Gamma_0
  for (j in 1:L) {                                  # Bartlett-weighted lags
    w  <- 1 - j / (L + 1)
    Xu <- X * u
    G  <- crossprod(Xu[(j+1):n, , drop = FALSE], Xu[1:(n-j), , drop = FALSE])
    meat <- meat + w * (G + t(G))
  }
  sqrt(diag(bread %*% meat %*% bread))
}
L <- 20
hand <- hac_se(ols, L)["SP500"]
out  <- c(OLS_se = summary(ols)$coefficients["SP500","Std. Error"],
          HAC_hand = unname(hand))
if (pk["sandwich"]) out["HAC_NeweyWest"] <- sqrt(sandwich::NeweyWest(ols, lag = L)["SP500","SP500"])
out["ratio_HAC_over_OLS"] <- out["HAC_hand"] / out["OLS_se"]
kable(t(round(out, 5)), caption = "Standard error of the slope: OLS vs HAC.")
if (pk["lmtest"] && pk["sandwich"])
  lmtest::coeftest(ols, vcov = sandwich::NeweyWest(ols, lag = L))
```

# Long-memory models {#sec-longmemory}

> *Slides §2.11.* Fractional differencing $(1-B)^d x_t=a_t$, $0<d<0.5$, gives a
> stationary series whose ACF decays **hyperbolically**. Returns are short
> memory ($d\approx0$); their **volatility proxies** are long memory.

The contrast is stark: $r_t$ decorrelates immediately, but $|r_t|$ and $r_t^2$
stay autocorrelated for hundreds of lags.

```{r lm-acf, fig.height=3.6}
L <- 100
ar_abs <- samp_acf(abs(sp_d), L); ar_sq <- samp_acf(sp_d^2, L); ar_r <- samp_acf(sp_d, L)
plot(0:L, ar_abs, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 2, ylim = c(-.05, .4),
     xlab = "lag (days)", ylab = "ACF", main = "S&P 500: returns vs |returns| vs squared")
lines(0:L, ar_sq, col = "tomato", lwd = 2); lines(0:L, ar_r, col = "grey50")
abline(h = 0); legend("topright", c("|r|","r^2","r"),
       col = c("steelblue","tomato","grey50"), lwd = 2, bty = "n")
```

**GPH estimator of $d$, by hand** (log-periodogram regression), cross-checked
against `fracdiff` if installed:

```{r gph}
gph_d <- function(x, m = floor(length(x)^0.5)) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]; n <- length(x)
  I <- (Mod(fft(x - mean(x)))^2) / (2 * pi * n)     # periodogram
  j <- 1:m; lam <- 2 * pi * j / n
  reg <- lm(log(I[j + 1]) ~ I(-2 * log(2 * sin(lam / 2))))
  unname(coef(reg)[2])                              # slope estimates d
}
d_tab <- data.frame(series = c("|r|", "r^2", "r"),
                    GPH_d  = c(gph_d(abs(sp_d)), gph_d(sp_d^2), gph_d(sp_d)))
kable(d_tab, digits = 3, caption = "GPH long-memory estimates (d>0 => long memory).")

if (pk["fracdiff"]) {
  fd <- fracdiff::fracdiff(abs(sp_d), nar = 0, nma = 0)
  cat(sprintf("fracdiff d for |r| = %.3f (GPH gave %.3f)\n", fd$d, gph_d(abs(sp_d))))
}
```

`d ≈ 0.4` for `|r|` versus `d ≈ 0` for `r`: volatility has long memory even
though direction does not. This is precisely the empirical fact that motivates
the ARCH/GARCH machinery of Chapter 3.

# Key takeaways {#sec-takeaways}

1. **Stationarity is the licence to do inference.** Returns qualify; prices do
   not. One difference bridges the two.
2. **ACF/PACF classify; Ljung–Box tests.** AR ⇒ PACF cuts off; MA ⇒ ACF cuts
   off; ARMA ⇒ both tail off.
3. **Frequency matters.** Daily index returns carry a small negative AR(1);
   monthly returns are white noise. Don't over-model.
4. **Build, then check.** Use AIC/BIC and EACF for order, but the verdict is
   always white-noise residuals — not a big $R^2$.
5. **Unit roots demand differencing**, not detrending; the ADF test has a
   non-standard distribution.
6. **Serially correlated regression errors break OLS standard errors** — fix
   with HAC (Newey–West) or by modelling the error process.
7. **Returns are short memory; volatility is long memory.** That single
   asymmetry is the doorway to Chapter 3 (ARCH/GARCH).

*Reproduce everything with `quarto render FI362_Ch2_Supplement.qmd`. Change the
running series by swapping a column name — the code is column-agnostic.*
